text:
Been a while.  It's not so complicated — until it is.

To a first approximation, you clone whatever object looks most like what you want.

Eventually, you'll find that what you meant was that you wanted a copy (independent slots) rather than a clone (inherited slots) at which point, you'll tend to keep a bunch of prototype objects around which have the defaults for everything.

Later, as you start to have complex structures of objects, you'll learn that the right clone/copy semantics interacts with how shallow/deep you want things to be.  It gets messy, and the next thing you know, you're writing factory methods.  :scream:

elements.text:

To a first approximation, you clone whatever object looks most like what you want.

Eventually, you'll find that what you meant was that you wanted a copy (independent slots) rather than a clone (inherited slots) at which point, you'll tend to keep a bunch of prototype objects around which have the defaults for everything.

Later, as you start to have complex structures of objects, you'll learn that the right clone/copy semantics interacts with how shallow/deep you want things to be.  It gets messy, and the next thing you know, you're writing factory methods.

text: Interesting! The syntax reminds me of the way components for Vue.js can be defined as Objects (name, data, props, methods, …). I’ve watched the first video. What I don’t quite understand is how code from methods/actions are compiled to Java/Swift/etc. — like one example used a DOM API. My impression was that FOAM might be used for cross-platform code generation but I might be wrong. I see the benefit of defining “implementation-independent” models like that. But I still wonder what FOAM actually does with code.

elements.text: Interesting! The syntax reminds me of the way components for Vue.js can be defined as Objects (name, data, props, methods, …). I’ve watched the first video. What I don’t quite understand is how code from methods/actions are compiled to Java/Swift/etc. — like one example used a DOM API. My impression was that FOAM might be used for cross-platform code generation but I might be wrong. I see the benefit of defining “implementation-independent” models like that. But I still wonder what FOAM actually does with code.

text: The syntax is just JSON + functions (although, you can also use XML syntax or GUI syntax, since models are modelled and you can use MVC to decouple their view from their representation). Yes, FOAM is used for cross-platform code generation. This short video shows more of that: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S4LbUv5FsGQ> For Javascript, since it is a very dynamic language, the class and prototype implementations are created from object and function composition at runtime. No code is ever actually generated. This is what keeps the download size small. For Java and Swift we statically generate code as part of the build process.  One of the things' that's unusual about FOAM is that it isn't entirely "implementation-independent". It doesn't mind being merely a 95-98% solution and then leave you to write the last little bit potentially in multiple languages. By not trying to be a 100% solution, it can be much smaller, and the result seems more natural on each target platform. See: <https://github.com/foam-framework/foam/wiki/How-FOAM-is-Different>

text: A few weeks ago someone convinced me to give the Remarkable 2 a shot. It's an eInk screen with a stylus (in the same way the iPhone is a phone with a touch screen). I absolutely love this thing. My new favorite "Tool for Thought". Highly recommend!

elements.text: A few weeks ago someone convinced me to give the Remarkable 2 a shot. It's an eInk screen with a stylus (in the same way the iPhone is a phone with a touch screen). I absolutely love this thing. My new favorite "Tool for Thought". Highly recommend!

text: Previously I had dozens of paper notebooks. I just did a 7 day trip with just the Remarkable and probably won't look back. All my chicken scratch drawings sync seamlessly to my devices. It's great for note taking, experiments, modeling, or just thinking!

text:
I got one a couple of weeks ago, I use a CLI tool to upload pdfs that are "printed" versions of web articles, most times using the readability view on firefox before printing.

Then I can go read, highlight and add notes on the margins without distraction.

The tool is this one: <https://gitlab.com/wrobell/remt/>

text:
If I wanted to build a slack bot with a future of coding technology, what alternatives do I have?

I want to build a bot that when someone posts in this slack in some channels the bot will be notified, will check if it has details about that user (mostly a link to some place on the web where people can find out more about them), if it has details or already contacted that user then it does nothing

if the user is new  and wasn't contacted then it sends a DM to the user asking to provide a public url to use to link to on the newsletter or just ignore the message.

What's your FoC stack? :slightly_smiling_face:

text: Hello Everyone! My name is Keith and I'm a computer scientist and member of the <https://www.wolframphysics.org/|Wolfram Physics Project>! I'm currently researching in creating science for the "Game of Life" !!! I'm quite excited to talk with some of you guys, heterodox programmers !!! I found the podcast Future of programming because I listened to Devine Lu Linvega at a conference at MILA(Montreal Institute of Learning Algorithms) Montréal. I'm quite interested in researching the creativity of computer programmers and I'm currently doing it with the 50 years of Game of Life development  !!

text: I think I might start calling myself a "Software designer" :sunglasses: <http://tomasp.net/blog/2021/software-designers/|http://tomasp.net/blog/2021/software-designers/>

attachments.title: Software designers, not engineers: An interview from alternative universe
attachments.text: How very different the world of software is in an alternative universe which has software designers rather than software engineers? In this interview, we look at a range of topics from software design sketching to software designer education, as well as the way in which software designers reshape any problem they are asked to solve.

text:
Yeah, I just started reading the book mentioned there, really enjoying it. You have to reinterpret words for it to be about software, but I think it fits quite well. 

While the division of three ways of knowing is of course a bit reductionist (and the authors admit so), these lists seems very right to me.
